analogy: Think of "Sexual Reproduction in Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: As you are aware, humans are sexually reproducing and viviparous. The reproductive events in humans include formation of gametes (gametogenesis), i.e., sperms in males and ovum in females, transfer of sperms into the female genital tract (insemination) and fusion of male and female gametes (fertilisation)... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2.1 THE MALE REPRODUCTIVE SYSTEM, CHAPTER 2, HUMAN REPRODUCTION, 2.1 The Male Reproductive. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Sexual Reproduction in Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that as you are aware, humans are sexually reproducing and viviparous.

The text explains that the reproductive events in humans include formation of gametes (gametogenesis), i.e., sperms in males and ovum in females, transfer of sperms into the female genital tract (insemination) and fusion of male and female gametes (fertilisation) leading to formation of zygote.

The text explains that this is followed by formation and development of blastocyst and its attachment to the uterine wall (implantation), embryonic development (gestation) and delivery of the baby (parturition).

The text explains that you have learnt that these reproductive events occur after puberty.

The text explains that there are remarkable differences between the reproductive events in the male and in the female, for example, sperm formation continues even in old men, but formation of ovum ceases in women around the age of fifty years.

The text explains that let us examine the male and female reproductive systems in human.

The text explains that 2.1 THE MALE REPRODUCTIVE SYSTEM The male reproductive system is located in the pelvis region (Figure 2.1a).

The text explains that it includes a pair of testes alongwith accessory ducts, glands and the external genitalia.

The text explains that cHAPTER 2 HUMAN REPRODUCTION 2.1 The Male Reproductive System 2.2 The Female Reproductive System 2.3 Gametogenesis 2.4 Menstrual Cycle 2.5 Fertilisation and Implantation 2.6 Pregnancy and Embryonic Development 2.7 Parturition and Lactation

The text explains that the testes are situated outside the abdominal cavity within a pouch called scrotum.

The text explains that the scrotum helps in maintaining the low temperature of the testes (2–2.5o C lower than the normal internal body temperature) necessary for spermatogenesis.

The text explains that in adults, each testis is oval in shape, with a length of about 4 to 5 cm and a width of about 2 to 3 cm.

The text explains that each testis has about 250 compartments called testicular lobules (Figure 2.1b).

The text explains that each lobule contains one to three highly coiled seminiferous tubules in which sperms are produced.

The text explains that each seminiferous tubule is lined on its inside by two types of cells called male germ cells (spermatogonia) and Sertoli cells (Figure 2.2 ).

The text explains that the male germ cells undergo meiotic divisions finally leading to sperm formation, while Sertoli cells provide nutrition to the germ cells.

The text explains that figure 2.1(a) Diagrammatic sectional view of male pelvis showing reproductive system Figure 2.1(b) Diagrammatic view of male reproductive system (part of testis is open to show inner details) 27 HUMAN REPRODUCTION

The text explains that 2.2 THE FEMALE REPRODUCTIVE SYSTEM The female reproductive system consists of a pair of ovaries alongwith a pair of oviducts, uterus, cervix, vagina and the external genitalia located in pelvic region (Figure 2.3a).

The text explains that these parts of the system alongwith a pair of the mammary glands are integrated structurally and functionally to support the processes of ovulation, fertilisation, pregnancy, birth and child care.

The text explains that the infundibulum leads to a wider Figure 2.3 (b) Diagrammatic sectional view of the female reproductive system Figure 2.3 (a) Diagrammatic sectional view of female pelvis showing reproductive system

The text explains that the clitoris is a tiny finger-like structure which lies at the upper junction of the two labia minora above the urethral opening.

The text explains that the mammary glands are paired structures (breasts) that contain glandular tissue and variable amount of fat.

The text explains that 2.3 GAMETOGENESIS The primary sex organs – the testis in the males and the ovaries in the females – produce gametes, i.e, sperms and ovum, respectively, by the process called gametogenesis.

The text explains that the spermatids are transformed into spermatozoa (sperms) by the process called spermiogenesis.

The text explains that after spermiogenesis, sperm heads become embedded in the Sertoli cells, and are finally released from the seminiferous tubules by the process called spermiation.

The text explains that androgens, in turn, stimulate the process of spermatogenesis.

The text explains that 32 BIOLOGY secretion of some factors which help in the process of spermiogenesis.

The text explains that it is a microscopic structure composed of a head, neck, a middle piece and a tail (Figure 2.6).

The text explains that the sperm head contains an elongated haploid nucleus, the anterior portion of which is covered by a cap-like structure, acrosome.

The text explains that the process of formation of a mature female gamete is called oogenesis which is markedly different from spermatogenesis.

The text explains that the Graafian follicle now ruptures to release the secondary oocyte (ovum) from the ovary by the process called ovulation.

The text explains that this is the reason why not all copulations lead to fertilisation and pregnancy.

The text explains that the process of fusion of a sperm with an ovum is called fertilisation.

The text explains that the zygote carrying XX would develop into a female baby and XY would form a male (you will learn more about the chromosomal patterns in Chapter 5).

The text explains that what are the major features of embryonic development at various months of pregnancy?

The text explains that by the end of 12 weeks (first trimester), most of the major organ systems are formed, for example, the limbs and external genital organs are well- developed.

The text explains that this process of delivery of the foetus (childbirth) is called parturition.

The text explains that the mammary glands of the female undergo differentiation during pregnancy and starts producing milk towards the end of pregnancy by the process called lactation.

The text explains that the male reproductive system is composed of a pair of testes, the male sex accessory ducts and the accessory glands and external genitalia.

The text explains that the female reproductive system consists of a pair of ovaries, a pair of oviducts, a uterus, a vagina, external genitalia, and a pair of mammary glands.

The text explains that the process of formation of mature female gametes is called oogenesis.

The text explains that the process of childbirth is called parturition which is induced by a complex neuroendocrine mechanism involving cortisol, estrogens and oxytocin.

The text explains that 40 BIOLOGY (f) The process of release of ovum from a mature follicle is called _____________ (g) Ovulation is induced by a hormone called _____________ (h) The fusion of male and female gametes is called _____________ (i) Fertilisation takes place in _____________ (j) Zygote divides to form _____________which is implanted in uterus.

The text explains that (k) The structure which provides vascular connection between foetus and uterus is called _____________ 2.

The text explains that draw a labelled diagram of male reproductive system.

The text explains that draw a labelled diagram of female reproductive system.

The text explains that describe the structure of a seminiferous tubule.

The text explains that briefly describe the process of spermatogenesis.

The text explains that (True/False) (d) Leydig cells synthesise androgens.

The text explains that (True/False) (e) Oogenesis takes place in corpus luteum.

The text explains that (True/False) (f) Menstrual cycle ceases during pregnancy.

The text explains that (True/False) (g) Presence or absence of hymen is not a reliable indicator of virginity or sexual experience.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2: Sexual Reproduction in Plants
